1:
On Hebrews 10:1 — “For the law having a shadow of good things to come, and not the very image of the things, can never with those sacrifices… make the comers thereunto perfect” — the witnesses mark the law’s shadow against the gospel’s substance.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figure. Robertson: the contrast of “shadow” (skian, shade) and “image” (eikon), Christ the eikon of God (Colossians 2:17). The Puritan Henry: the apostle lays the Levitical dispensation low when set against Christ. Wesley: a “bare, unsubstantial shadow… not the substantial, solid image.”

The verse states the law’s limitation. Having only a shadow of the good things to come, not their very image, the law’s repeated sacrifices could never perfect the worshippers. The shadow cannot do the work of the substance.

So the witnesses see the writer open the chapter by setting the law’s shadow against the gospel’s reality. The witnesses note the careful figure: the law had but “a shadow of good things to come” — a faint, outline foreshadowing of the blessings Christ would bring — “and not the very image,” not the solid substance itself. They mark that a shadow tells of the coming object but has none of its reality; the Levitical system pointed forward but could not embody the salvation it pictured. They observe the proof of its weakness: its sacrifices, “offered year by year continually,” could never “make the comers thereunto perfect” — never truly cleanse the conscience or complete the worshipper. They note Henry’s point that the law, excellent in its place, is laid low when set in competition with Christ, to whom it was only meant to lead. They observe Wesley’s contrast of the bare shadow and the solid image. The verse thus states the theme of the chapter: the law’s sacrifices, being only shadow, were inherently unable to perfect; their very repetition, as the next verse argues, betrayed their insufficiency — preparing the contrast with the one perfecting sacrifice of Christ.

So the witnesses see the law’s shadow set against the gospel’s substance. The law had but “a shadow of good things to come” — a faint outline foreshadowing Christ’s blessings — “and not the very image,” the solid substance itself. A shadow tells of the coming object but has none of its reality. So its sacrifices, “offered year by year continually,” could never “make the comers perfect.” Henry notes the law, excellent in its place, is laid low when set in competition with Christ. Wesley contrasts the bare shadow and the solid image. The shadow cannot do the work of the substance.
2:
On Hebrews 10:2 — “For then would they not have ceased to be offered? because that the worshippers once purged should have had no more conscience of sins” — the witnesses mark the argument from repetition.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the logic. Robertson: the conclusion of a second-class condition — had they perfected, they would have ceased. Barnes: the very repetition showed “some deficiency.” Ellicott: the repetition “a testimony to its imperfection.” Wesley: those once perfectly purged “would have been no longer conscious… of the guilt… of their sins.”

The verse argues from the sacrifices’ repetition. Had they truly perfected, they would have ceased; for the cleansed worshipper would feel no more guilt. Continual repeating proves continual insufficiency.

So the witnesses see the writer prove the law’s insufficiency by the very fact that its sacrifices were repeated. The witnesses note the logic: if the offerings had really perfected the worshippers, “would they not have ceased to be offered?” — a true and final cleansing would have ended the need for further sacrifice. They mark the test of true purgation: “the worshippers once purged should have had no more conscience of sins” — one truly cleansed would no longer feel the guilt and burden of sin pressing on the conscience; the work would be done once for all. They observe that, on the contrary, the sacrifices went on year after year, which betrays that they never reached this finality; the endless repeating is itself the confession of their failure. They note Ellicott’s point that the very repetition was a standing testimony to imperfection. They observe that the cleansed conscience — the relief from the sense of guilt — is the mark of a real atonement, which the Levitical rites never gave. The verse thus drives home the argument: the ceaseless repeating of the sacrifices proves they could not perfect; a finished work would have ended them — the contrast with Christ’s one finished offering being the writer’s aim.

So the witnesses see the law’s insufficiency proven by its very repetition. Had the offerings truly perfected, “would they not have ceased to be offered?” — a final cleansing would have ended the need. The test of true purgation: “the worshippers once purged should have had no more conscience of sins” — one truly cleansed would feel no more guilt pressing on the conscience. But the sacrifices went on year after year, betraying that they never reached finality. Ellicott’s point: the very repetition was a standing testimony to imperfection. The cleansed conscience is the mark of a real atonement, which the Levitical rites never gave.
3:
On Hebrews 10:3 — “But in those sacrifices there is a remembrance again made of sins every year” — the witnesses mark the annual reminder of sin.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the day of atonement. Barnes: the reference is to the great day of atonement, once a year. Robertson: “a remembrance” (anamnesis), a reminder. Ellicott: the high priest’s threefold confession of sin. The Reformed Calvin: sins were brought to remembrance “that guilt might be removed.”

The verse names what the sacrifices actually did. Instead of removing sin, they brought it yearly to remembrance. The annual atonement was a renewed confession of guilt, not its cancellation.

So the witnesses see the writer turn the very purpose of the yearly sacrifice into proof of its inadequacy. The witnesses note that, far from putting sin away, the annual atonement “made a remembrance… of sins every year” — each year’s observance was a fresh reminder, a renewed confession that sin still stood unremoved. They mark Ellicott’s detail of the day of atonement, when the high priest made solemn, repeated acknowledgment of sin — for himself, his house, and the people. They observe that this is the opposite of what a true atonement does: a real cleansing would put sin out of remembrance (as the new covenant promises, v. 17), but the Levitical rite kept calling it to mind. They note Wesley’s point that the yearly commemoration of the sins of that and all preceding years was a clear proof that the guilt was not perfectly purged. They observe Calvin’s sense that the sins were remembered so that guilt might be removed by the sacrifice then offered — yet only typically, pointing to the true removal in Christ. The verse thus deepens the argument: the annual sacrifice, by recalling sin year after year, confessed that it could not take it away — for, as the next verse states plainly, the blood of beasts cannot remove sin.

So the witnesses see the yearly sacrifice turn its own purpose into proof of inadequacy. Far from putting sin away, the annual atonement “made a remembrance of sins every year” — each observance a fresh reminder that sin still stood unremoved. Ellicott details the day of atonement, when the high priest made solemn, repeated acknowledgment of sin. This is the opposite of true atonement, which puts sin out of remembrance (v17); the Levitical rite kept calling it to mind. Wesley: the yearly commemoration was a clear proof that guilt was not perfectly purged. Calvin: sins were remembered so guilt might be removed — yet only typically, pointing to Christ.
4:
On Hebrews 10:4 — “For it is not possible that the blood of bulls and of goats should take away sins” — the witnesses mark the impossibility at the heart of the argument.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the absolute. Robertson: “should take away” (aphairein) sins. Adam Clarke: “common sense must have taught them” that animal blood could not satisfy justice or cleanse the conscience. Ellicott: the offering was necessary and answered its purpose, but could not remove sin. The Reformed Calvin: the blood of beasts was a symbol and pledge of the real cleansing.

The verse states the decisive reason. It is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins. No animal sacrifice can do what only Christ can. The whole Levitical system is judged by this.

So the witnesses see the writer ground the law’s insufficiency in a flat impossibility. The witnesses note the absolute statement: “it is not possible” — not merely difficult or insufficient, but utterly impossible — that the blood of animals should “take away sins.” They mark Clarke’s point that common sense itself must have shown this: the death of beasts could never satisfy divine justice nor cleanse the guilty conscience, and God intended the worshippers to understand it so. They observe the proper place the sacrifices held, which the expositors are careful to preserve: the offerings were divinely appointed, necessary in their season, and answered their typical purpose — but their purpose was to point beyond themselves, not to atone in themselves. They note Calvin’s sense that the blood of beasts was a symbol and pledge of the real cleansing to come in Christ, a shadow of the true sacrifice. They observe that this verse is the hinge of the whole argument: since animal blood cannot remove sin, the endless sacrifices could only foreshadow, and a better, real sacrifice was needed. The verse thus declares the impossibility that the gospel answers: the blood of bulls and goats cannot take away sin — which is why, as the next verses show, Christ came to do God’s will by the offering of his own body.
5:
On Hebrews 10:5 — “Wherefore when he cometh into the world, he saith, Sacrifice and offering thou wouldest not, but a body hast thou prepared me” — the witnesses mark Christ’s word at his coming.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the citation. Robertson: Christ at his Incarnation quoting Psalm 40:6–8; the LXX reads “a body” (soma) where the Hebrew has “ears.” Adam Clarke: the Messiah “about to be incarnated” declares God never willed the legal sacrifices to atone. The Reformed Calvin: his coming into the world is his manifestation in the flesh.

The verse puts Psalm 40 in Christ’s mouth at his incarnation. God desired not sacrifice and offering, but prepared a body for his Son. The true offering is the incarnate Christ himself.

So the witnesses see the writer apply Psalm 40 to Christ entering the world. The witnesses note the setting: “when he cometh into the world” — the moment of the Incarnation, when the Son took human nature and appeared among men; Christ is represented as speaking these prophetic words at his coming. They mark the substance: “Sacrifice and offering thou wouldest not” — God never truly willed the legal sacrifices as the means of atonement; they could not satisfy or please him. They observe the great clause: “but a body hast thou prepared me” — in place of the rejected animal offerings, God prepared for his Son a human body, the true sacrifice to be offered. They note the textual point the expositors raise: the Hebrew of the Psalm reads “ears hast thou digged (opened) for me,” while the Septuagint, which the writer follows, has “a body hast thou prepared me” — the sense being consonant, for the opened ear of obedience and the prepared body of the obedient Servant both point to the willing self-offering of Christ. They observe that this grounds the whole gospel answer in the Incarnation: God set aside the sacrifices and prepared a body for his Son to offer. The verse thus opens the great quotation by which the writer shows that Christ came to do God’s will by offering himself — the body prepared for sacrifice.
6:
On Hebrews 10:6 — “In burnt offerings and sacrifices for sin thou hast had no pleasure” — the witnesses mark God’s displeasure in the legal offerings.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the four offerings. Robertson: God took no pleasure in the animal offering, meal-offering, burnt-offering, sin-offering. Ellicott: “whole burnt offerings,” the symbol of complete consecration. Adam Clarke: God could never be pleased with the legal victims as atonements, for they could not satisfy his justice.

The verse repeats the divine verdict. In whole burnt offerings and sin offerings God had no pleasure. The fullest of the legal sacrifices still failed to please him.

So the witnesses see the citation press God’s lack of pleasure in the legal offerings. The witnesses note the comprehensiveness: the quotation names the whole range of sacrifices — the slain offering, the meal-offering, the “burnt offerings” (the whole burnt offering, the symbol of entire consecration), and the “sacrifices for sin” (the sin-offering) — covering every kind of legal sacrifice. They mark the verdict on them all: “thou hast had no pleasure” — God found no real satisfaction, no delight, in any of them as the ground of atonement. They observe Clarke’s point that God could never be pleased with the legal victims as making atonement, since they could neither satisfy his justice nor honor his law. They note that this does not mean the sacrifices were valueless in their place — they were appointed and typical — but that, as the means of removing sin and pleasing God, they were inadequate; God’s pleasure lay elsewhere, in the obedience of his Son. They observe that even the most complete offering, the whole burnt sacrifice of total consecration, did not avail. The verse thus underlines the insufficiency of the entire sacrificial system: in none of its offerings did God take pleasure as the true atonement — preparing the contrast with the willing obedience Christ now declares.

So the witnesses see the citation press God’s lack of pleasure in the legal offerings. The quotation names the whole range — the slain offering, the meal-offering, the “whole burnt offerings” (the symbol of entire consecration), the “sacrifices for sin” — covering every kind. The verdict on them all: “thou hast had no pleasure.” Clarke’s point: God could never be pleased with the legal victims as making atonement, since they could not satisfy his justice or honour his law. Not that they were valueless in their typical place, but as the means of removing sin they were inadequate; God’s pleasure lay in the obedience of his Son. Even the whole burnt sacrifice of total consecration did not avail.
7:
On Hebrews 10:7 — “Then said I, Lo, I come (in the volume of the book it is written of me) to do thy will, O God” — the witnesses mark Christ’s self-offering to do God’s will.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the obedience. Robertson: the Messiah offers himself to do God’s will (the genitive infinitive of purpose). Adam Clarke: “in the roll of the book,” the ancient scroll. The Puritan Henry: the apostle exalts Christ as the true high priest and atoning sacrifice. Wesley: “I come to do thy will — by the sacrifice of myself.”

The verse gives Christ’s answer to the failure of sacrifices. Since they could not please God, the Son comes to do God’s will. His obedient self-offering replaces the legal offerings.

So the witnesses see the climax of the quotation: Christ comes to do God’s will. The witnesses note the “then”: when it was plain that the legal sacrifices could not please God, the Son says, “Lo, I come.” They mark the prophetic warrant: “in the volume of the book it is written of me” — in the scroll of Scripture (the “roll” in which ancient books were written) it stood recorded that the Messiah should come; Christ’s coming fulfills the written word. They observe the heart of it: “to do thy will, O God” — the Son presents himself to accomplish the Father’s will, the genitive marking purpose; and that will, as the next verses show, was the offering of his own body. They note Wesley’s concise reading: he comes to do God’s will by the sacrifice of himself. They observe that this is the great contrast: where the animal offerings failed because they were not the obedience God sought, Christ comes in willing obedience, offering not the blood of beasts but himself. The verse thus sets the obedient self-offering of Christ in the place of the rejected sacrifices: the Son comes, as the Scriptures foretold, to do the Father’s will — the abolishing of the first system and the establishing of the second, which the writer now draws out.

So the witnesses see the climax of the quotation: Christ comes to do God’s will. The “then”: when it was plain the legal sacrifices could not please God, the Son says, “Lo, I come.” The prophetic warrant: “in the volume of the book it is written of me” — in the scroll of Scripture it stood recorded that the Messiah should come. The heart of it: “to do thy will, O God” — the Son presents himself to accomplish the Father’s will, the genitive marking purpose. Wesley: he comes to do God’s will by the sacrifice of himself. Where the animal offerings failed because they were not the obedience God sought, Christ comes in willing obedience.
8:
On Hebrews 10:8 — “Above when he said, Sacrifice and offering… thou wouldest not, neither hadst pleasure therein; which are offered by the law” — the witnesses mark the writer’s exposition of the quotation.

The Eastern fathers note the answer to an objection. Chrysostom: he meets the question, if the sacrifices are types, why have they not ceased? The grammatical-historical Robertson: Christ “saying above” (anoteron legon), quoting vv. 5–6 again. Ellicott notes the plural “sacrifices and offerings.” Barnes: the former part of the quotation said God did not require sacrifices.

The verse begins the writer’s comment on the Psalm. He repeats that God neither willed nor delighted in the legal sacrifices. The first part of the quotation sets aside the old offerings.

So the witnesses see the writer take up the quotation he has given and draw out its force in two stages. The witnesses note that he first recalls the opening words: “Sacrifice and offering… thou wouldest not, neither hadst pleasure therein” — the Psalm’s rejection of the legal sacrifices, which he has just cited. They mark the pointed addition: “which are offered by the law” — identifying these as the very sacrifices the Mosaic law prescribed, so that the Psalm itself, part of Israel’s own Scriptures, declares God’s dissatisfaction with the law’s offerings. They observe Chrysostom’s point that this answers a natural objection: if the sacrifices were only types, why did they continue after the truth came? The answer is that the Psalm foretold their setting aside. They note that this first stage of the comment establishes the negative side: God did not will the legal sacrifices; they are, by his own word, set aside. They observe that this prepares the positive stage of the next verse, where Christ’s coming to do God’s will establishes the new in place of the old. The verse thus opens the exposition: the Psalm, rightly read, declares that the legal sacrifices — those the law itself commanded — were neither willed nor pleasing to God, and so were to be removed.

So the witnesses see the writer draw out the quotation in two stages. First he recalls the opening words: “Sacrifice and offering thou wouldest not, neither hadst pleasure therein” — the Psalm’s rejection of the legal sacrifices. The pointed addition: “which are offered by the law” — identifying these as the very sacrifices Moses prescribed, so that Israel’s own Scripture declares God’s dissatisfaction with the law’s offerings. Chrysostom’s point: this answers the objection why the types continued after the truth came — the Psalm foretold their setting aside. This first stage establishes the negative side: God did not will the legal sacrifices; by his own word they are set aside, preparing the positive stage to follow.
9:
On Hebrews 10:9 — “Then said he, Lo, I come to do thy will, O God. He taketh away the first, that he may establish the second” — the witnesses mark the abolishing of the old and establishing of the new.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the conclusion. Robertson: “he taketh away the first” (anairei to proton, present active) and establishes the second. Adam Clarke: the first is the legal offerings; the second, “the offering of the body of Jesus once for all.” The Reformed Calvin: the perfect rule of God’s will is set up when the sacrifices are removed.

The verse draws the great conclusion. By coming to do God’s will, Christ takes away the first system to establish the second. The old sacrifices are abolished; the obedient self-offering is set up.

So the witnesses see the writer complete his exposition: Christ’s coming abolishes the old and establishes the new. The witnesses note the second stage of the comment: where the Psalm first set aside the sacrifices, it then declares the Son’s coming “to do thy will” — and the writer draws the decisive inference. They mark the two movements: “he taketh away the first” — he removes, abolishes, the first thing, the whole system of legal sacrifices that could not please God; “that he may establish the second” — that he may set up the second, the doing of God’s will by the offering of Christ’s own body. They observe Clarke’s identification: the first is the legal offerings, the second the one offering of Jesus once for all. They note Calvin’s point that when the sacrifices are removed, the will of God is set up as the perfect rule, so that the righteousness of Christ’s kingdom needs no Levitical sacrifices. They observe the significance: the very Scripture the Jews revered foretold the abolition of their sacrificial system and its replacement by Christ’s obedience. The verse thus crowns the argument from Psalm 40: Christ, by his obedient self-offering, takes away the old covenant’s sacrifices to establish the new — the will of God done once for all, by which, as the next verse declares, believers are sanctified.
10:
On Hebrews 10:10 — “By the which will we are sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all” — the witnesses mark the believer’s sanctification by the one offering.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the means. Robertson: “we have been sanctified” (hegiasmenoi esmen, periphrastic perfect) — the divine will realized in Christ’s offering. Ellicott: Christ did God’s will in his sacrifice of obedience even unto death (Philippians 2:8). Wesley: “cleansed from guilt, and consecrated to God.”

The verse states the saving result. By that will of God, fulfilled in Christ, believers are sanctified through the once-for-all offering of his body. The Son’s obedient sacrifice cleanses and consecrates the people.

So the witnesses see the writer apply Christ’s obedience to the believer’s sanctification. The witnesses note the ground: “by the which will” — by that will of God which Christ came to do, fulfilled in his self-offering; the divine will, which the animal sacrifices could not accomplish, is realized in Christ. They mark the result: “we are sanctified” — the perfect tense marking a settled, accomplished state; believers have been set apart, cleansed from guilt, and consecrated to God. They observe the means: “through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all” — the body God prepared (v. 5), offered in obedient sacrifice on the cross, and offered “once for all,” not repeated like the legal sacrifices. They note the contrast that runs through the chapter: the many ineffectual offerings of the law against the one effectual offering of Christ. They observe that this verse gathers up the whole argument from Psalm 40 into its saving point: it is by Christ’s doing of God’s will, in the once-for-all offering of his body, that believers are truly sanctified — the perfecting the law could never give. The verse thus states the gospel result of Christ’s obedience: the one offering of his body sanctifies the people of God, decisively and forever.

So the witnesses see the writer apply Christ’s obedience to the believer’s sanctification. The ground: “by the which will” — that will of God which Christ came to do, fulfilled in his self-offering; the divine will the animal sacrifices could not accomplish is realized in Christ. The result: “we are sanctified” — the perfect tense marking a settled state; believers set apart, cleansed from guilt, consecrated to God. The means: “through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all” — the prepared body, offered in obedient sacrifice, not repeated like the legal offerings. The many ineffectual offerings of the law against the one effectual offering of Christ — the perfecting the law could never give.
11:
On Hebrews 10:11 — “And every priest standeth daily ministering and offering oftentimes the same sacrifices, which can never take away sins” — the witnesses mark the standing priest and the unavailing sacrifices.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the contrast of posture. Robertson: “standeth” (hesteken), a vivid picture; the priest ministering and offering. Adam Clarke: the Jewish priest stands daily, repeating the same sacrifices; our High Priest sat down. The Reformed Calvin: daily sacrificing is foreign to Christ’s priesthood.

The verse pictures the Levitical priest at his endless work. He stands daily, offering the same sacrifices that can never take sin away. The unfinished posture matches the unfinished work.

So the witnesses see the writer set the standing, ever-laboring priest against the seated Christ. The witnesses note the vivid posture: “every priest standeth daily ministering” — the Levitical priest stands, the posture of a servant whose work is never done, day after day at the altar. They mark the repetition: “offering oftentimes the same sacrifices” — the same offerings, over and over, with no finality; the endless sameness betrays that nothing is accomplished. They observe the damning verdict, repeated from earlier in the chapter: these sacrifices “can never take away sins” — for all their multiplication, they leave sin unremoved. They note the contrast the expositors draw, which the next verse completes: the standing priest, ever busy and never finished, against the High Priest who, having offered one sacrifice, sat down. They observe Calvin’s point that the practice of daily sacrificing is wholly foreign to Christ’s priesthood, which rests on one finished work. The verse thus sharpens the contrast: the Levitical priest’s perpetual standing and endless offering, fruitless against sin, throws into relief the once-for-all, completed offering of Christ — who, as the next verse declares, sat down forever.

So the witnesses see the standing, ever-labouring priest set against the seated Christ. The vivid posture: “every priest standeth daily ministering” — the posture of a servant whose work is never done, day after day at the altar. The repetition: “offering oftentimes the same sacrifices” — the same offerings over and over, the endless sameness betraying that nothing is accomplished. The verdict, repeated from earlier: these “can never take away sins.” The contrast the next verse completes: the standing priest, ever busy and never finished, against the High Priest who, having offered one sacrifice, sat down. Calvin: daily sacrificing is wholly foreign to Christ’s priesthood, which rests on one finished work.
12:
On Hebrews 10:12 — “But this man, after he had offered one sacrifice for sins for ever, sat down on the right hand of God” — the witnesses mark the one sacrifice and the seated Christ.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the contrast. Robertson: “one sacrifice” (mian thysian), the main point; the single act doing what the many could not. Ellicott: a combination of Hebrews 7:27 and Hebrews 8:1, with “for ever” added. Wesley: he “sat down — as a son, in majesty and honour.”

The verse sets Christ against the Levitical priest. He offered one sacrifice for sins forever, then sat down at God’s right hand. The finished work is shown by the seated posture.

So the witnesses see the writer crown the contrast: Christ offered one sacrifice and sat down. The witnesses note the emphatic “but this man” (or “but he”) — set against the many standing priests. They mark the singularity: “one sacrifice for sins” — not the endless repetition of the law, but a single offering, which (as the chapter has shown) does what the many could never do; the “one” is the heart of the matter. They observe the permanence: “for ever” — the one sacrifice avails perpetually, its efficacy never exhausted nor needing renewal. They note the decisive posture: “sat down on the right hand of God” — the Levitical priest stood, his work unfinished; Christ sat down, the posture of completion and of royal majesty, his atoning work done. They observe Wesley’s point that he sat down as a Son, in honor and majesty, not as a servant still laboring. They note that the seated Christ at God’s right hand declares the atonement finished and accepted. The verse thus completes the great contrast: against the standing priest with his endless, unavailing sacrifices stands the seated Christ, who by one sacrifice for sins forever has finished the work and taken his place of honor — from which, as the next verse adds, he awaits his final triumph.

So the witnesses see the writer crown the contrast: Christ offered one sacrifice and sat down. The emphatic “but this man,” set against the many standing priests. The singularity: “one sacrifice for sins” — not the endless repetition of the law, but a single offering doing what the many never could; the “one” is the heart of the matter. The permanence: “for ever” — its efficacy never exhausted nor needing renewal. The decisive posture: “sat down on the right hand of God” — the Levitical priest stood, his work unfinished; Christ sat down, the posture of completion and royal majesty. Wesley: he sat down as a Son, in honour, not as a servant still labouring. The seated Christ declares the atonement finished and accepted.
13:
On Hebrews 10:13 — “From henceforth expecting till his enemies be made his footstool” — the witnesses mark Christ’s expectant waiting for final victory.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the allusion. Robertson: “henceforth expecting” (to loipon ekdechomenos), the attitude of final, certain victory (1 Corinthians 15:25); the citation of Psalm 110:1. Adam Clarke: till all who oppose his priesthood are defeated. Ellicott: he waits for the promised subjection of his foes.

The verse pictures the seated Christ waiting. Having finished his sacrifice, he expects the final subjection of his enemies. The atoning work done, the victory is now assured.

So the witnesses see the seated Christ awaiting the consummation of his triumph. The witnesses note the contrast continued from the standing priest: Christ does not stand to offer again, but “from henceforth” — for the future — sits “expecting,” waiting in the calm assurance of certain victory; his redemptive work is complete, and only its final outworking remains. They mark the citation of Psalm 110: “till his enemies be made his footstool” — the great Messianic promise that the exalted Lord shall reign until every foe is subdued beneath his feet. They observe Clarke’s point that this includes all who oppose his priesthood and sacrifice, who shall be defeated and confounded. They note that the posture of expectation befits the finished atonement: there is nothing more for Christ to do for the removal of sin; he waits only for the promised subjection of his enemies, the gathering of his redeemed and the overthrow of all opposition. They observe that this is the confident attitude of one whose final triumph is sure. The verse thus completes the picture of the seated Christ: having offered the one sacrifice and sat down, he waits in royal certainty for the day when all his foes are made his footstool — for, as the next verse states, by that one offering he has perfected his people forever.

So the witnesses see the seated Christ awaiting the consummation of his triumph. He does not stand to offer again, but “from henceforth” sits “expecting,” waiting in the calm assurance of certain victory; his redemptive work is complete, and only its final outworking remains. The citation of Psalm 110: “till his enemies be made his footstool” — the great Messianic promise that the exalted Lord shall reign until every foe is subdued. Clarke: this includes all who oppose his priesthood and sacrifice. The posture of expectation befits the finished atonement: there is nothing more for Christ to do for the removal of sin; he waits only for the promised subjection of his foes, in royal certainty.
14:
On Hebrews 10:14 — “For by one offering he hath perfected for ever them that are sanctified” — the witnesses mark the perfecting power of the one offering.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the achievement. Robertson: “he hath perfected” (teteleioken, perfect active) — he has done what the old sacrifices failed to do. Adam Clarke: to make perfect is to procure remission of sins and holiness. Ellicott: “perfection” that came not through the law now comes by the one offering. Barnes: he has laid the foundation for eternal perfection.

The verse states the crowning result. By one offering Christ has perfected forever those who are sanctified. The single sacrifice accomplishes a complete and lasting perfection.

So the witnesses see the writer sum up the whole argument: the one offering perfects forever. The witnesses note the contrast with the law, which “can never make perfect” (v. 1): Christ “hath perfected” — the perfect tense marking a completed, abiding achievement. They mark the means: “by one offering” — the single sacrifice of himself, against the law’s endless repetition; what the many offerings could not do, the one offering has done. They observe the completeness and permanence: “for ever” — the perfecting is not partial or temporary but full and lasting; those once perfected by Christ’s offering need no further sacrifice. They note the expositors’ explanation of “perfected”: it means the procuring of complete remission of sins and the consecration to holiness, the very perfection the Levitical system could not give. They observe the description of the people: “them that are sanctified” — those set apart and being made holy by Christ’s offering. They note that this verse is the triumphant climax of the chapter’s doctrine: the one sacrifice has accomplished, once for all, the perfecting of God’s people that the many sacrifices never could. The verse thus seals the argument: by his single offering Christ has perfected his people forever — a finished work the Holy Spirit himself, as the next verses show, attests in the new covenant.

So the witnesses see the writer sum up the whole argument: the one offering perfects forever. The contrast with the law, which “can never make perfect” (v1): Christ “hath perfected” — the perfect tense marking a completed, abiding achievement. The means: “by one offering” — the single sacrifice, against the law’s endless repetition. The permanence: “for ever” — full and lasting, those once perfected needing no further sacrifice. The expositors explain “perfected”: complete remission of sins and consecration to holiness, the very perfection the Levitical system could not give. The people: “them that are sanctified.” The triumphant climax of the chapter’s doctrine: the one sacrifice has accomplished, once for all, what the many never could.
15:
On Hebrews 10:15 — “Whereof the Holy Ghost also is a witness to us: for after that he had said before” — the witnesses mark the Spirit’s testimony.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the appeal. Robertson: “the Holy Ghost beareth witness to us,” confirming the interpretation by Jeremiah (Jeremiah 31:33), already quoted (Hebrews 8:8). Adam Clarke: Jeremiah spoke by inspiration of the Spirit. Wesley: he winds up the argument, describing the new covenant now ratified.

The verse calls the Holy Spirit as witness. The Spirit, speaking in Scripture, attests the truth of the finished sacrifice. Jeremiah’s prophecy is the divine confirmation.

So the witnesses see the writer summon the Holy Spirit as witness to the doctrine of the one perfecting sacrifice. The witnesses note the appeal: “the Holy Ghost also is a witness to us” — the Spirit, who spoke through the prophets in Scripture, bears testimony to the truth just established. They mark the high view of Scripture implied: the words of Jeremiah, which the writer is about to quote again, are the very witness of the Holy Spirit; the prophet spoke by inspiration, so that to cite Jeremiah is to hear the Spirit. They observe that the writer repeats the new-covenant prophecy he had given earlier (in chapter 8), now to confirm specifically that the one sacrifice has secured the forgiveness that ends all further offering. They note Wesley’s point that here the apostle winds up his whole argument on the excellency of Christ’s priesthood and sacrifice, describing the new covenant as now fully ratified. They observe that the appeal to the Spirit lends divine authority to the conclusion: it is not the writer’s inference only, but the testimony of God himself in his word. The verse thus introduces the clinching proof: the Holy Spirit, in the prophecy of the new covenant, witnesses that by Christ’s one offering sins are forgiven — the covenant promise the next verses recite.

So the witnesses see the writer summon the Holy Spirit as witness to the one perfecting sacrifice. The appeal: “the Holy Ghost also is a witness to us” — the Spirit, who spoke through the prophets in Scripture, bears testimony to the truth just established. A high view of Scripture is implied: the words of Jeremiah, about to be quoted again, are the very witness of the Spirit; the prophet spoke by inspiration. Wesley: here the apostle winds up his whole argument on the excellency of Christ’s priesthood, describing the new covenant as now fully ratified. The appeal to the Spirit lends divine authority to the conclusion: not the writer’s inference only, but the testimony of God himself in his word.
16:
On Hebrews 10:16 — “This is the covenant that I will make with them after those days, saith the Lord, I will put my laws into their hearts, and in their minds will I write them” — the witnesses mark the new covenant’s inward law.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the citation. Robertson: the writer quotes Jeremiah 31:33 as before (Hebrews 8:10), changing only the order of “heart” and “mind.” Ellicott notes the abridgment of the longer prophecy. The law written within is the mark of the new covenant.

The verse recites the new covenant promise. God will write his laws on the heart and mind. The inward law marks the covenant Christ has ratified.

So the witnesses see the writer recite the heart of the new covenant: the inward law. The witnesses note that he quotes Jeremiah’s prophecy, already given in chapter 8, now to seal the argument about the one sacrifice. They mark the great promise: “I will put my laws into their hearts, and in their minds will I write them” — under the new covenant God writes his law not on tablets of stone, external and condemning, but within, on the heart and mind, so that obedience flows from an inward principle of grace. They observe that this inward writing is the fruit of the sanctification just described: those perfected by Christ’s offering have the law inscribed within them by the Spirit. They note the writer’s slight rearrangement (heart before mind, or the reverse), which does not alter the sense. They observe that the covenant is “made with them after those days” — the new age inaugurated by Christ, replacing the old. They note that the new covenant’s inward law is one half of its promise; the other, the forgiveness of sins, follows in the next verse and is the point the writer drives toward. The verse thus recites the new covenant’s first great blessing: the law written on the heart — the inward transformation that accompanies the forgiveness secured by Christ’s one sacrifice.

So the witnesses see the writer recite the heart of the new covenant: the inward law. He quotes Jeremiah’s prophecy, already given in chapter 8, now to seal the argument. The great promise: “I will put my laws into their hearts, and in their minds will I write them” — God writes his law not on tablets of stone, external and condemning, but within, so that obedience flows from an inward principle of grace. This inward writing is the fruit of the sanctification just described: those perfected by Christ’s offering have the law inscribed within them by the Spirit. The covenant is “made with them after those days” — the new age inaugurated by Christ. The inward law is one half of the promise; forgiveness follows in the next verse.
17:
On Hebrews 10:17 — “And their sins and iniquities will I remember no more” — the witnesses mark the promise of full forgiveness.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the climax. Robertson: the writer adds “iniquities” to “sins” of Hebrews 8:12, with the emphatic future. Gill: God’s non-remembrance of sin is the remission of sin. Ellicott notes the writer reaches toward his triumphant conclusion.

The verse gives the covenant’s crowning promise. God will remember their sins no more. Full and final forgiveness is the heart of the new covenant.

So the witnesses see the writer fix on the new covenant’s crowning promise: the forgiveness of sins. The witnesses note the great word: “their sins and iniquities will I remember no more” — God promises not merely to overlook but to remember sin no more, to blot it from his reckoning entirely. They mark Gill’s point that God’s non-remembrance of sin is precisely the remission of sin: to be remembered no more is to be wholly and finally forgiven. They observe the sharp contrast with the old system: where the yearly sacrifice made “a remembrance… of sins every year” (v. 3), the new covenant promises sins remembered no more — the very opposite, full pardon replacing perpetual recall. They note that this is the promise the writer has been driving toward, for it is the proof that the one sacrifice has truly removed sin: if God remembers it no more, it is gone. They observe the completeness of the forgiveness — both “sins” and “iniquities,” the whole guilt, put away forever. The verse thus states the new covenant’s deepest blessing, secured by Christ’s one offering: God remembers his people’s sins no more — a full forgiveness from which, as the next verse concludes, it follows that no further sacrifice for sin is needed.

So the witnesses see the writer fix on the new covenant’s crowning promise: the forgiveness of sins. The great word: “their sins and iniquities will I remember no more” — God promises not merely to overlook but to remember sin no more, to blot it from his reckoning entirely. Gill’s point: God’s non-remembrance of sin is precisely the remission of sin; to be remembered no more is to be wholly forgiven. The sharp contrast: where the yearly sacrifice made “a remembrance of sins every year” (v3), the new covenant promises sins remembered no more — full pardon replacing perpetual recall. Both “sins” and “iniquities,” the whole guilt, put away forever — the proof that the one sacrifice has truly removed sin.
18:
On Hebrews 10:18 — “Now where remission of these is, there is no more offering for sin” — the witnesses mark the triumphant conclusion.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the inference. Robertson: “the logical and triumphant conclusion” — there is actually remission (aphesis, removal) of sins. Adam Clarke: where sin is once pardoned, there is no further need of a sin-offering. Ellicott: here the argument reaches its triumphant close, opening the last great division of the epistle.

The verse draws the decisive conclusion. Where sins are forgiven, no further offering for sin remains. The completeness of forgiveness ends all sacrifice.

So the witnesses see the writer reach the triumphant conclusion of his whole argument on the sacrifice. The witnesses note the logic: “where remission of these is” — where the sins and iniquities just named are forgiven, remembered no more — “there is no more offering for sin.” They mark the force of it: if sin is truly and finally pardoned, no further sacrifice can be needed or possible; a debt fully paid cannot be paid again. They observe Clarke’s point that where sin is once pardoned, there is no more need of a sin-offering — the believer in Christ has his sin blotted out and needs no other offering. They note that this overthrows the whole rationale of the repeated Levitical sacrifices: their endless repetition assumed sin unremoved; but Christ’s one offering has secured full remission, so the sacrifices are not only superseded but rendered needless. They observe Ellicott’s point that here the argument reaches its triumphant close, completing the great doctrinal section and opening the exhortations that follow. The verse thus seals the chapter’s doctrine: the forgiveness won by Christ’s one sacrifice is so complete that no further offering for sin remains — the finished work on which the writer now builds his urgent appeal to draw near.

So the witnesses see the writer reach the triumphant conclusion of his argument on the sacrifice. The logic: “where remission of these is” — where the sins just named are forgiven, remembered no more — “there is no more offering for sin.” The force: if sin is truly and finally pardoned, no further sacrifice can be needed or possible; a debt fully paid cannot be paid again. Clarke: where sin is once pardoned, there is no more need of a sin-offering. This overthrows the whole rationale of the repeated Levitical sacrifices, whose repetition assumed sin unremoved. Ellicott: here the argument reaches its triumphant close, opening the exhortations. The forgiveness won by Christ’s one sacrifice is so complete that no further offering remains.
19:
On Hebrews 10:19 — “Having therefore, brethren, boldness to enter into the holiest by the blood of Jesus” — the witnesses mark the turn from doctrine to exhortation.

The Eastern fathers note the structure. Chrysostom: having shown the difference of priest, sacrifice, tabernacle, and covenant, he draws the practical conclusion. The grammatical-historical Robertson: the second resume of the argument (Hebrews 4:14–16) with earnest exhortation. The Puritan Henry: the dignities of the gospel state — boldness to enter the holiest. The Reformed Calvin: all ceremonies barring access are removed.

The verse begins the great exhortation. By Christ’s blood believers have boldness to enter the holiest. The finished sacrifice opens the way into God’s presence.

So the witnesses see the writer turn from the doctrine of the sacrifice to its glorious practical fruit: access to God. The witnesses note the “therefore” — on the ground of the finished, perfecting sacrifice just expounded, the exhortation now begins; the doctrinal section gives way to the hortatory. They mark the privilege: “boldness to enter into the holiest” — the very inmost sanctuary, the Holy of Holies, into which under the law only the high priest could go, once a year; now every believer has free, confident entrance. They observe the means: “by the blood of Jesus” — it is Christ’s atoning blood, the one sacrifice, that opens the way and gives the boldness; the access rests wholly on his finished work. They note Chrysostom’s and Robertson’s point that this resumes and applies the whole foregoing argument about the superior priest, sacrifice, and covenant. They observe Calvin’s sense that all the ceremonial barriers to God’s presence are now removed. They note the warm address, “brethren,” engaging affection. The verse thus opens the exhortation with the gospel’s crowning privilege: through Christ’s blood, believers may enter God’s very presence with boldness — the access the next verses describe and the first of the three “let us” appeals build upon.

So the witnesses see the writer turn from doctrine to its glorious fruit: access to God. The “therefore” — on the ground of the finished, perfecting sacrifice, the exhortation begins. The privilege: “boldness to enter into the holiest” — the inmost sanctuary, into which under the law only the high priest could go, once a year; now every believer has free, confident entrance. The means: “by the blood of Jesus” — the access rests wholly on his finished work. Chrysostom and Robertson note this resumes the whole argument about the superior priest, sacrifice, and covenant. Calvin: all ceremonial barriers are now removed. Through Christ’s blood, believers may enter God’s very presence with boldness.
20:
On Hebrews 10:20 — “By a new and living way, which he hath consecrated for us, through the veil, that is to say, his flesh” — the witnesses mark the new and living way.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figures. Robertson: “new” (prosphaton, freshly made) and “living” (zosan), opened by Christ’s Incarnation and Death. Adam Clarke: a new way, none had entered heaven before; the veil rent is his flesh. Wesley: “by rending the veil… the holy of holies became accessible.” The Reformed Calvin: the divinity, hid in Christ’s flesh, leads us into heaven.

The verse describes the way of access. Christ has opened a new and living way through the veil of his flesh. His incarnate body, offered in death, is the path into God’s presence.

So the witnesses see the writer describe the access in two rich figures: a new and living way through the veil. The witnesses note the first: “a new and living way” — “new,” freshly opened, a way not known or possible before, for none had entered the heavenly sanctuary until Christ; and “living,” not a dead path but one that gives and sustains life, perhaps because Christ the living Lord is himself the way. They mark that he “consecrated” or dedicated it for us — opened and inaugurated the way by his own work. They observe the second figure: “through the veil, that is to say, his flesh” — as the temple veil shut off the Holy of Holies, so Christ’s flesh both veiled the divine glory and, being rent in death (as the temple veil was torn), opened the way into God’s presence; access is through his crucified body. They note Calvin’s point that the Godhead, hidden in Christ’s flesh, leads us into heaven, so that none finds God but through the man Christ. They observe the wonder: the very flesh that veiled God becomes, in its rending, the door. The verse thus unfolds the way of access: a new, living way that Christ has opened through the veil of his own flesh — the incarnate, crucified Saviour himself the path into the holiest.
21:
On Hebrews 10:21 — “And having an high priest over the house of God” — the witnesses mark the great High Priest over God’s house.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the title. Robertson: “a great priest” (hierea megan), as shown in Hebrews 4:14–7:28; “over the house of God” as God’s Son (Hebrews 3:6). Adam Clarke: the house is the Christian church; Christ offers his blood and their prayers. Barnes: the spiritual house, the church.

The verse adds the second ground of confidence. Believers have a great High Priest over God’s house. Christ’s priestly rule completes the assurance of access.

So the witnesses see the writer name the second great ground of the believer’s boldness: a great High Priest. The witnesses note that, besides the new and living way (the means of access), believers have “an high priest” — or, more strictly, “a great priest” — one who, as the epistle has shown at length, surpasses all the Levitical priests, having offered the one perfect sacrifice and entered the heavenly sanctuary. They mark his domain: “over the house of God” — set over God’s household, the church, not merely as a servant (as Moses) but as a Son. They observe Clarke’s point that over this house Christ presides as High Priest, offering his own blood for his people and presenting their prayers and praises to God. They note that this priestly headship completes the believer’s confidence: not only is the way opened, but a faithful, sympathizing High Priest stands ever in God’s presence on his people’s behalf. They observe that the “house of God” is the spiritual temple, the company of believers over whom Christ rules and for whom he intercedes. The verse thus adds the second pillar of assurance: a great High Priest over God’s house — so that, with the way opened and the Priest interceding, believers are summoned, in the next verses, to draw near with full confidence.

So the witnesses see the writer name the second ground of the believer’s boldness: a great High Priest. Besides the new and living way, believers have “an high priest” — more strictly “a great priest” — one who surpasses all the Levitical priests, having offered the one perfect sacrifice and entered the heavenly sanctuary. His domain: “over the house of God” — set over the church, not as a servant like Moses but as a Son. Clarke: over this house Christ presides, offering his own blood and presenting his people’s prayers and praises. This priestly headship completes the believer’s confidence: not only is the way opened, but a faithful, sympathizing High Priest stands ever in God’s presence on his people’s behalf.
22:
On Hebrews 10:22 — “Let us draw near with a true heart in full assurance of faith, having our hearts sprinkled from an evil conscience, and our bodies washed with pure water” — the witnesses mark the first exhortation.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the appeal. Robertson: “let us draw near” (proserchometha, volitive subjunctive), the first of three exhortations (Hebrews 4:16). Adam Clarke: the language is sacrificial — come with the blood, in full assurance. Wesley: hearts sprinkled from an evil conscience, bodies washed with pure water. The Reformed Calvin: what we bring must correspond to Christ’s spiritual sacrifice.

The verse gives the first “let us.” Draw near to God with a true heart and full assurance, cleansed within and without. The opened way is to be used in worship.

So the witnesses see the writer issue the first of three exhortations: draw near. The witnesses note that the access secured by Christ is not to be left unused; believers are summoned to “draw near” to God — in prayer, worship, and communion — the very thing the opened way and the High Priest make possible. They mark the manner: “with a true heart” — in sincerity, without hypocrisy; and “in full assurance of faith” — with confident, unwavering trust, resting wholly on Christ’s finished work. They observe the twofold cleansing, drawn from the sacrificial and priestly washings: “our hearts sprinkled from an evil conscience” — the inner man cleansed by Christ’s blood from the guilt that accuses, so the conscience no longer condemns; and “our bodies washed with pure water” — the outer life purified, perhaps with allusion to baptism, the whole person consecrated. They note Calvin’s point that what we bring must answer to the spiritual nature of Christ’s sacrifice: a cleansed heart and life. They observe that this exhortation gathers up the privilege of access into a duty of worship. The verse thus gives the first great appeal: let believers, cleansed by Christ within and without, draw near to God with sincere hearts and full assurance of faith — the worship the opened way invites.
23:
On Hebrews 10:23 — “Let us hold fast the profession of our faith without wavering; (for he is faithful that promised)” — the witnesses mark the second exhortation.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the term. Robertson: “let us hold fast” (katechomen) “that it waver not” (akline); “a confession of hope, not of despair.” Ellicott: better, “our hope,” not “faith.” The Reformed Calvin: he mentions hope rather than faith, born of and sustained by faith. Wesley: “the hope which we professed at our baptism.”

The verse gives the second “let us.” Hold fast the confession of hope unwaveringly, for God is faithful. Steadfast confession answers the faithful Promiser.

So the witnesses see the writer give the second exhortation: hold fast the confession. The witnesses note that the believers, then under persecution and in danger of falling away, are charged to “hold fast” — to keep a firm grip on — “the profession,” or open confession, of their faith and hope. They mark the qualification: “without wavering” — unbending, not leaning this way or that, steadfast against the pressures that would make them let go. They observe the expositors’ note that the better reading is the confession of “hope,” not “faith” — the hope, born of faith and sustained by it, which the believer professes (Calvin and Wesley relating it to the hope confessed at baptism). They note the ground of steadfastness given: “he is faithful that promised” — God, who has made the promises of the new covenant, is utterly faithful and will keep them; the believer’s confession need not waver, for it rests on a God who cannot fail. They observe that this answers wavering with the faithfulness of the Promiser: since God is sure, the confession of hope may be held firm. The verse thus gives the second appeal: let believers hold fast their confession of hope without wavering, anchored in the faithfulness of God who promised — perseverance grounded in the divine fidelity.

So the witnesses see the writer give the second exhortation: hold fast the confession. The believers, under persecution and in danger of falling away, are charged to “hold fast” — keep a firm grip on — the open confession of their faith and hope. The qualification: “without wavering” — unbending, not leaning this way or that, steadfast against the pressures that would make them let go. The better reading is the confession of “hope,” born of faith and sustained by it, which Calvin and Wesley relate to the hope confessed at baptism. The ground of steadfastness: “he is faithful that promised” — God will keep his covenant promises; the confession need not waver, for it rests on a God who cannot fail.
24:
On Hebrews 10:24 — “And let us consider one another to provoke unto love and to good works” — the witnesses mark the third exhortation, to mutual care.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the term. Robertson: “let us consider one another” (katanoomen allelous); “to provoke” (eis paroxysmon), our “paroxysm,” to stimulate. Adam Clarke: attentively consider each other’s trials, to excite love and good works. The Reformed Calvin addresses the Jews’ arrogance, urging mutual regard.

The verse gives the third “let us.” Consider one another so as to stir up love and good works. Mutual attention spurs the community to godliness.

So the witnesses see the writer turn from individual duties to mutual care: consider one another. The witnesses note the movement from the personal (draw near, hold fast) to the communal: believers are to “consider one another” — to give attentive, thoughtful regard to their fellow-Christians, their needs, trials, weaknesses, and temptations. They mark the aim: “to provoke unto love and to good works” — the word “provoke” (whence our “paroxysm”) means to stimulate, incite, spur; here turned to a good end, a holy provocation to love and good deeds. They observe the striking redemption of a word usually negative: instead of provoking one another to anger or rivalry, believers are to stir each other up to love and active goodness. They note Clarke’s point that this means attentively considering one another’s circumstances so as to excite mutual love and fruitfulness. They observe that the Christian life is not solitary: believers are responsible for one another’s growth, and much may be done by mutual encouragement. The verse thus gives the third appeal: let believers watch over one another, not selfishly but with thoughtful care, to incite each other to love and good works — a mutual stirring-up that requires, as the next verse insists, the keeping of their assemblies.

So the witnesses see the writer turn from individual duties to mutual care: consider one another. The movement from the personal (draw near, hold fast) to the communal: believers are to “consider one another” — to give attentive, thoughtful regard to their fellow-Christians, their needs, trials, and temptations. The aim: “to provoke unto love and to good works” — the word “provoke” (whence our “paroxysm”) means to stimulate, spur; here turned to a good end. The striking redemption of a usually negative word: instead of provoking one another to anger, believers stir each other up to love and active goodness. Clarke: attentively considering one another’s circumstances to excite mutual love and fruitfulness. The Christian life is not solitary.
25:
On Hebrews 10:25 — “Not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together, as the manner of some is; but exhorting one another: and so much the more, as ye see the day approaching” — the witnesses mark the charge to keep the assemblies.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the term and the danger. Robertson: “not forsaking” (me egkataleipontes, not leaving in the lurch); “the assembling” (episynagogen, 2 Thessalonians 2:1). Ellicott: some had drifted back toward the synagogue. Wesley: forsaking through fear of persecution or a vain notion of being above ordinances. The Reformed Calvin: the assembly increased by additions.

The verse warns against forsaking the assembly. Believers are not to abandon their meetings, as some did, but to exhort one another, the more as the day nears. Communal worship sustains perseverance.

So the witnesses see the writer make mutual care concrete: do not forsake the assembly. The witnesses note the warning: “not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together” — not abandoning the gathered worship and fellowship of the church, the very setting in which believers stir one another up. They mark the sober observation: “as the manner of some is” — already some had fallen into the habit of staying away, whether (as Wesley suggests) through fear of persecution, or from a vain notion of being above ordinances, or, as Ellicott notes, drifting back toward the synagogue. They observe the positive duty: “but exhorting one another” — the assembly is for mutual encouragement, the believers building each other up. They note the urgent motive: “and so much the more, as ye see the day approaching” — the more the day draws near (the day of judgment, or of impending crisis upon Jerusalem), the more needful the gathering and mutual exhortation. They observe that the forsaking of the assembly is no small fault but a step toward the apostasy the next verses warn against; to abandon the fellowship is to expose oneself to falling away. The verse thus completes the exhortation to mutual care with its practical demand: keep the assembling, exhort one another, and the more as the day approaches — for to forsake the meeting is to court the wilful sin against which the writer now solemnly warns.
26:
On Hebrews 10:26 — “For if we sin wilfully after that we have received the knowledge of the truth, there remaineth no more sacrifice for sins” — the witnesses mark the solemn warning against wilful apostasy.

The Eastern fathers note the image. Chrysostom: trees that yield no return after all care are “pulled up by the roots, and handed over to the fire.” The grammatical-historical commentators define the sin. Robertson: “if we sin wilfully” (hekousios hamartanonton); a state of wilful, continued sin. Wesley: “by total apostasy from God… termed drawing back.” The Puritan Henry: a total and final falling away, rejecting Christ. Barnes: apostasy, leaving no recovery.

The verse warns of the sin that has no remedy. To sin wilfully after knowing the truth leaves no further sacrifice. Deliberate apostasy from Christ forfeits the only atonement.

So the witnesses see the writer turn from exhortation to solemn warning against the sin that has no remedy. The witnesses are careful, with the expositors, to define the sin precisely: “if we sin wilfully after… the knowledge of the truth” means not ordinary sins of infirmity, nor even gross falls from which one may be recovered, but the deliberate, total, and final apostasy — the renouncing of Christ and the gospel after having known and professed them, what the chapter later calls “drawing back.” They mark the dreadful consequence: “there remaineth no more sacrifice for sins” — for the apostate, having rejected the one sacrifice of Christ (the only sacrifice that can save), there is no other to which he can turn; he has cast away the sole remedy. They observe Chrysostom’s image of the fruitless tree rooted up and burned. They note that this is not a denial of forgiveness to the penitent, but a warning that to apostatize from Christ is to abandon the only ground of pardon, for there is no second atonement. They observe that the warning is addressed to those who had “received the knowledge of the truth” — making the sin the more heinous. The verse thus opens the chapter’s grave warning: wilful, final apostasy from the known truth of Christ leaves no sacrifice remaining — only the fearful judgment the next verses describe.
27:
On Hebrews 10:27 — “But a certain fearful looking for of judgment and fiery indignation, which shall devour the adversaries” — the witnesses mark the apostate’s dreadful expectation.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the term. Robertson: “expectation” (ekdoche) of judgment, perhaps “coined by the writer”; the “fiery indignation” devouring the adversaries. Ellicott: for Christ’s waiting servants judgment is lost in salvation; to these is left only the awaiting of judgment. The Reformed Calvin: the torment of an evil conscience.

The verse describes what remains for the apostate. Not a sacrifice but a fearful expectation of judgment and devouring fire. Having forsaken Christ, he awaits only wrath.

So the witnesses see the writer set forth the only thing that remains for the apostate: dreadful judgment. The witnesses note the contrast with the believer: where Christ’s waiting servants look for salvation (v. 13; 9:28), the apostate has nothing but “a certain fearful looking for of judgment” — a terrified expectation of the coming reckoning, the conscience already anticipating its doom. They mark the imagery: “fiery indignation, which shall devour the adversaries” — the consuming fire of God’s wrath that will destroy those who have set themselves against him, recalling the language of judgment in the prophets. They observe Calvin’s point that this includes the present torment of an evil conscience, which not only lacks grace but knows it has tasted and lost it forever through its own fault — a foretaste of the judgment to come. They note that the apostate, by rejecting the one sacrifice, has made himself an “adversary” of God, and so falls under the fire reserved for his enemies. They observe that the warning is meant to alarm and so to preserve. The verse thus continues the solemn warning: for the wilful apostate there remains no sacrifice but only a fearful expectation of judgment and the devouring fire that consumes God’s adversaries — a doom the next verses show to be far worse than that under the law.

So the witnesses see the writer set forth the only thing that remains for the apostate: dreadful judgment. The contrast with the believer: where Christ’s waiting servants look for salvation (v13; 9:28), the apostate has nothing but “a certain fearful looking for of judgment” — a terrified expectation of the coming reckoning, the conscience already anticipating its doom. The imagery: “fiery indignation, which shall devour the adversaries” — the consuming fire of God’s wrath. Calvin: this includes the present torment of an evil conscience, which knows it has tasted and lost grace forever through its own fault. The apostate, by rejecting the one sacrifice, has made himself an “adversary” of God, and so falls under the fire reserved for his enemies.
28:
On Hebrews 10:28 — “He that despised Moses’ law died without mercy under two or three witnesses” — the witnesses mark the penalty under the law.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the reference. Robertson: “hath set at naught” (athetesas), to render void; “without mercy” (choris oiktirmon). Ellicott: the reference is to Deuteronomy 17:2–7, apostasy from Jehovah. Adam Clarke: died without any mitigation, on the testimony of two or three witnesses (Numbers 15:30). The Reformed Calvin: an argument from the less to the greater.

The verse recalls the law’s severity. He who set aside Moses’ law died without mercy on the testimony of witnesses. The old covenant punished apostasy with death.

So the witnesses see the writer establish the lesser case as the ground of an argument from the smaller to the greater. The witnesses note the offense: “he that despised Moses’ law” — or set it at naught, rendered it void; the reference, as Ellicott notes, is especially to the law against apostasy and idolatry in Deuteronomy, the deliberate, presumptuous rejection of God’s covenant. They mark the penalty: “died without mercy” — the offender suffered death, with no mitigation, no second chance, no pardon; the law showed no clemency to the convicted apostate. They observe the safeguard of justice: “under two or three witnesses” — the sentence was passed on the testimony of the required witnesses, so that it was no arbitrary cruelty but legal justice. They note Calvin’s point that this is an argument from the less to the greater (the first of the rabbinic rules of interpretation): if the violation of the law of Moses brought death without mercy, how much heavier the punishment for rejecting the gospel. They observe that the writer cites the severity of the old covenant precisely to make the warning under the new the more terrible. The verse thus lays the ground for the greater warning: the apostate under Moses died without mercy on the witnesses’ testimony — and how much sorer, the next verse asks, the punishment of him who tramples the Son of God.

So the witnesses see the writer establish the lesser case for an argument from the smaller to the greater. The offence: “he that despised Moses’ law” — set it at naught, rendered it void; the reference especially to the law against apostasy and idolatry in Deuteronomy, the deliberate rejection of God’s covenant. The penalty: “died without mercy” — death with no mitigation or pardon; the law showed no clemency to the convicted apostate. The safeguard: “under two or three witnesses” — legal justice, not arbitrary cruelty. Calvin: this is an argument from the less to the greater — if violating Moses’ law brought death without mercy, how much heavier the punishment for rejecting the gospel, which the next verse asks.
29:
On Hebrews 10:29 — “Of how much sorer punishment… who hath trodden under foot the Son of God, and hath counted the blood of the covenant… an unholy thing, and hath done despite unto the Spirit of grace?” — the witnesses mark the threefold guilt of the apostate.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the argument. Robertson: “how much” (instrumental of degree), an argument from less to greater; “sorer” (cheironos), worse. Adam Clarke: trodden the Son of God under foot with utmost contempt; the blood of the covenant counted unholy. The Reformed Calvin: a sorer punishment — not bodily death only but eternal perdition. Wesley: this refers to wilful, total apostasy alone.

The verse measures the apostate’s greater guilt. He tramples the Son of God, profanes the covenant blood, and insults the Spirit. Such guilt deserves a far sorer punishment.

So the witnesses see the writer drive home the greater warning by naming the apostate’s threefold sin. The witnesses note the question of degree: “how much sorer punishment” — if despising Moses’ law brought death, far worse must befall the one who rejects the gospel; Calvin specifies that this is not bodily death only but eternal perdition. They mark the three heinous offenses against the three Persons and the gospel itself. First, he “hath trodden under foot the Son of God” — trampled with utter contempt the Lord of glory, treating the Saviour as worthless. Second, he “hath counted the blood of the covenant… an unholy thing” — reckoned the precious atoning blood, by which he was once set apart, as common, profane, no different from any other; the very blood that ratified the new covenant despised. Third, he “hath done despite unto the Spirit of grace” — insulted and outraged the Holy Spirit, who brings grace, by rejecting his work. They observe Wesley’s and the expositors’ insistence that this describes only the wilful, total apostate, not the ordinary sinner. They note the climactic horror: to spurn the Son, profane his blood, and insult the Spirit is the deepest of all impieties. The verse thus completes the argument: such threefold guilt against the gospel deserves a punishment far sorer than the law’s — for it falls into the hands of the living God.
30:
On Hebrews 10:30 — “For we know him that hath said, Vengeance belongeth unto me, I will recompense, saith the Lord. And again, The Lord shall judge his people” — the witnesses mark the certainty of God’s vengeance.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the citations. Robertson: God lives and is true to his word, quoting Deuteronomy 32:35 (cf. Romans 12:19) and Deuteronomy 32:36. Adam Clarke: God’s vengeance on his enemies. The Reformed Calvin: “the Lord shall judge his people” — he will rigorously judge those who rebel. Wesley: far more rigorously than the heathen.

The verse grounds the warning in God’s own word. Vengeance and recompense belong to the Lord, who will judge his people. The Judge is sure to punish apostasy.

So the witnesses see the writer confirm the warning by appeal to God’s own declared character. The witnesses note the certainty: “we know him that hath said” — the readers know the God who speaks in Scripture, the living and true God who keeps his word. They mark the first citation: “Vengeance belongeth unto me, I will recompense” — God claims the right and power of retribution; he will repay the apostate, for justice is his. They observe the second: “The Lord shall judge his people” — God will bring his own professing people to judgment, and (as Calvin and Wesley note) will deal the more rigorously with those who, having known him, rebel; the nearness of the covenant aggravates rather than excuses the sin. They note that both quotations come from Deuteronomy 32, the song of Moses, fittingly drawn from the law to confirm the warning to Jewish believers. They observe that this is no idle threat: it rests on the revealed nature of God as the just avenger. They note that to know this God is to know that apostasy cannot escape. The verse thus undergirds the warning with God’s own word: vengeance is his, and he will judge his people — making it, as the next verse declares, a fearful thing to fall into his hands.

So the witnesses see the writer confirm the warning by appeal to God’s declared character. The certainty: “we know him that hath said” — the readers know the God who speaks in Scripture, the living and true God who keeps his word. The first citation: “Vengeance belongeth unto me, I will recompense” — God claims the right of retribution; he will repay the apostate. The second: “The Lord shall judge his people” — God will bring his own professing people to judgment, and (Calvin, Wesley) will deal the more rigorously with those who, having known him, rebel. Both quotations come from Deuteronomy 32, fittingly drawn from the law to confirm the warning to Jewish believers. To know this God is to know that apostasy cannot escape.
31:
On Hebrews 10:31 — “It is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God” — the witnesses mark the dread conclusion of the warning.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the force. Robertson: “a fearful thing” (phoberon); “the sense is not to be explained away… the wrath of God faces wrongdoers.” Adam Clarke: to fall under his displeasure, and he who lives forever can punish forever. Ellicott: “a living God,” whose power to punish is unending.

The verse states the dread truth. It is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God. The apostate faces the wrath of the eternal, almighty God.

So the witnesses see the writer close the warning with a statement of awful weight. The witnesses note the solemnity: “it is a fearful thing” — terrifying, dreadful; the writer will not soften it, for, as Robertson insists, the sense is not to be explained away — the wrath of God truly faces the wrongdoer. They mark the object of dread: “to fall into the hands of the living God” — to come under the avenging power of God, not for mercy but for judgment; the apostate, having rejected grace, falls into the grasp of the very God he has spurned. They observe the force of “the living God” — not a dead idol that cannot punish, but the eternal, almighty God who lives forever and therefore (as Clarke notes) can punish forever; his displeasure resting on the soul is an unending doom. They note that to fall into the hands of God for judgment is the most fearful of all conceivable fates, for there is no escape from omnipotence and no end to eternity. They observe that this verse is the climax of the warning, designed to strike awe and so to keep the wavering from apostasy. The verse thus seals the solemn warning: the wilful apostate faces the most dreadful of ends — to fall into the avenging hands of the living, eternal God — from which the writer turns, in the next verses, to encouragement.

So the witnesses see the writer close the warning with a statement of awful weight. The solemnity: “it is a fearful thing” — terrifying, dreadful; the writer will not soften it, for, as Robertson insists, the sense is not to be explained away — the wrath of God truly faces the wrongdoer. The object of dread: “to fall into the hands of the living God” — to come under the avenging power of God, not for mercy but for judgment. The force of “the living God”: not a dead idol that cannot punish, but the eternal, almighty God who lives forever and therefore (Clarke) can punish forever. To fall into the hands of God for judgment is the most fearful of all fates, for there is no escape from omnipotence and no end to eternity.
32:
On Hebrews 10:32 — “But call to remembrance the former days, in which, after ye were illuminated, ye endured a great fight of afflictions” — the witnesses mark the turn from warning to encouragement.

The Eastern fathers note the method. Chrysostom: the best physicians, after a deep incision, soothe the wound rather than cut again. The grammatical-historical Robertson: “call to remembrance” (anamimneskesthe), recalling earlier sufferings (Hebrews 5:12). Ellicott: from warning he turns to encouragement, as in chapter 6. Wesley: “illuminated — with the knowledge of God.”

The verse turns to encouragement by memory. Recall the former days when, newly enlightened, you endured great affliction. Their past steadfastness is held up to rouse present endurance.

So the witnesses see the writer, having warned severely, now turn to encourage by reminding the readers of their earlier faithfulness. The witnesses note Chrysostom’s beautiful image of the wise physician who, after a deep incision, soothes rather than cuts again — so the writer, having alarmed, now comforts.
