They mark the appeal to memory: “call to remembrance the former days” — recall the time soon after conversion, when their zeal was fresh. They observe the description of their conversion: “after ye were illuminated” — enlightened with the knowledge of God and the gospel, brought from darkness to light. They note the trial they then bore: “ye endured a great fight of afflictions” — a hard, prolonged conflict of sufferings, persecutions endured for the faith soon after they believed. They observe that the writer holds up their own past steadfastness as both an encouragement and a reproach: those who endured so much at the first should not now grow weary and draw back. They note Calvin’s point that it is shameful to begin well and faint midway, worse still to retrograde. The verse thus opens the encouragement: let them remember how, when first enlightened, they bravely endured great affliction — a memory meant to rekindle their endurance, which the next verses detail.

So the witnesses see the writer, having warned severely, turn to encourage by recalling earlier faithfulness. Chrysostom’s image: the wise physician who, after a deep incision, soothes rather than cuts again. The appeal to memory: “call to remembrance the former days” — recall the time soon after conversion, when zeal was fresh. The description: “after ye were illuminated” — enlightened with the knowledge of God, brought from darkness to light. The trial: “ye endured a great fight of afflictions” — a hard, prolonged conflict of sufferings soon after they believed. Their past steadfastness is held up as both encouragement and reproach. Calvin: shameful to begin well and faint midway, worse to retrograde.
33:
On Hebrews 10:33 — “Partly, whilst ye were made a gazingstock both by reproaches and afflictions; and partly, whilst ye became companions of them that were so used” — the witnesses mark the two forms of their suffering.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the imagery. Robertson: “being made a gazing-stock” (theatrizomenoi), brought upon the stage to derision (cf. 1 Corinthians 4:9). Adam Clarke: exhibited as a spectacle in the theatre. Ellicott: probably figurative. The Puritan Henry: the Christian spirit pities and helps others in suffering.

The verse details their afflictions. They were themselves made a public spectacle of reproach, and they shared the sufferings of others so treated. They suffered both directly and by sympathy.

So the witnesses see the writer specify the two ways the readers had suffered. The witnesses note the first: “made a gazingstock… by reproaches and afflictions” — they were exposed to public view, held up to scorn as if on a stage in a theatre, the objects of insults and ill-treatment for their faith; the word pictures a spectacle of derision. They mark the second: “ye became companions of them that were so used” — they did not only suffer in their own persons, but voluntarily made themselves partners with others who were so treated, sharing their reproach and standing by them in their afflictions. They observe Henry’s point that the Christian spirit is not selfish but pities, visits, helps, and pleads for the suffering; the readers had shown this generous fellowship in others’ trials. They note that this double suffering — bearing their own reproach and sharing others’ — marked the courage and love of their early faith. They observe that to become a companion of the persecuted, when it was dangerous and shameful to do so, was itself a brave confession. The verse thus details their former endurance: made a public spectacle of reproach themselves, and willingly sharing the sufferings of their persecuted brethren — a steadfast love and courage the writer recalls to shame any present faintness.

So the witnesses see the writer specify the two ways the readers had suffered. The first: “made a gazingstock… by reproaches and afflictions” — exposed to public view, held up to scorn as if on a stage in a theatre, the objects of insults for their faith. The second: “ye became companions of them that were so used” — they did not only suffer in their own persons, but voluntarily made themselves partners with others so treated, sharing their reproach. Henry: the Christian spirit is not selfish but pities, visits, and helps the suffering. To become a companion of the persecuted, when dangerous and shameful, was itself a brave confession — the courage and love of their early faith.
34:
On Hebrews 10:34 — “For ye had compassion of me in my bonds, and took joyfully the spoiling of your goods, knowing… that ye have in heaven a better and an enduring substance” — the witnesses mark their joyful sacrifice.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the reading and the joy. Robertson: “ye had compassion on” (synepathesate), with the better text “them that were in bonds” (tois desmiois). Ellicott notes the true reading, “them that were in bonds.” Wesley: they sympathized with their suffering brethren and received joyfully the loss of their goods. The hope of a better, enduring possession sustained them.

The verse recalls their costly love. They had compassion on prisoners and bore the plunder of their goods with joy, knowing they had a better, lasting possession in heaven. Heavenly hope made earthly loss a joy.

So the witnesses see the writer recall the readers’ costly and joyful endurance. The witnesses note their compassion: they “had compassion” on the imprisoned — the better text reads “them that were in bonds,” the prisoners for the faith — sympathizing with and helping them, at risk to themselves, in their captivity. They mark the remarkable spirit in which they bore their own loss: “took joyfully the spoiling of your goods” — when their property was seized or plundered for their faith, they accepted it not with grief or resentment but with joy. They observe the ground of that joy: “knowing… that ye have in heaven a better and an enduring substance” — they knew they possessed a heavenly inheritance, better and lasting, beside which the loss of earthly goods was nothing; the eternal substance outweighed the perishable. They note that this hope is the secret of cheerful suffering: those sure of a better, abiding possession can let the temporal go without dismay. They observe that such joyful endurance under loss was a high mark of faith. The verse thus completes the record of their former steadfastness: they pitied prisoners and gladly bore the plunder of their goods, sustained by the certain hope of a better, enduring possession in heaven — a hope the writer would have them keep, that they cast not away their confidence.
35:
On Hebrews 10:35 — “Cast not away therefore your confidence, which hath great recompence of reward” — the witnesses mark the charge to keep confidence.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the term. Robertson: “cast not away your boldness” (me apobalete ten parresian), throw away as worthless. Adam Clarke: do not throw away your liberty of access, your birthright as sons. Ellicott: the opposite of “holding fast the boldness of the hope” (Hebrews 3:6). The Reformed Calvin: confidence is the foundation of a godly life.

The verse exhorts to retain confidence. Do not throw away your confidence, which carries a great reward. Boldness in faith is to be kept, not surrendered.

So the witnesses see the writer apply the memory of their steadfastness to a present charge: do not cast away your confidence. The witnesses note the figure: to “cast away” is to throw off as worthless, to discard; the readers are warned not to fling away the “confidence” or boldness — the bold, free trust toward God and the open profession of faith — that they had shown so bravely at the first. They mark Clarke’s sense that this confidence is their liberty of access to God, their birthright as his sons, and the assurance of his favor; to throw it away is a grievous loss. They observe the strong motive: “which hath great recompence of reward” — this confidence, held fast, brings a great reward; perseverance is not in vain but richly repaid. They note Calvin’s point that confidence is the very foundation of a godly and holy life, and that to lose it is to lose the reward set before us. They observe that the charge fits the moment: having endured so much, the readers must not now, under fresh pressure, surrender the boldness that endurance had proved. The verse thus turns the encouragement into exhortation: do not throw away the hard-won confidence of faith, for it carries a great reward — a perseverance that needs, as the next verse says, patience.

So the witnesses see the writer apply the memory of their steadfastness to a present charge: do not cast away your confidence. The figure: to “cast away” is to throw off as worthless, to discard; the readers are warned not to fling away the “confidence” or boldness they had shown so bravely at first. Clarke: this confidence is their liberty of access to God, their birthright as sons. The motive: “which hath great recompence of reward” — held fast, it brings a great reward; perseverance is richly repaid. Calvin: confidence is the very foundation of a godly life, and to lose it is to lose the reward. Having endured so much, they must not now surrender the boldness endurance had proved.
36:
On Hebrews 10:36 — “For ye have need of patience, that, after ye have done the will of God, ye might receive the promise” — the witnesses mark the need of patient endurance.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the term. Robertson: “of patience” (hypomones), endurance; “recompense of reward” (misthapodosian, Hebrews 11:6). Adam Clarke: God furnishes the grace; you must exercise it. Ellicott: brave, patient endurance (Hebrews 6:12). The Reformed Calvin: patience is necessary, for Satan harasses us by many arts.

The verse states the present need. They need patience, that having done God’s will they may receive the promise. Endurance bridges the doing and the receiving.

So the witnesses see the writer name the grace the readers now most need: patient endurance. The witnesses note the necessity: “ye have need of patience” — not that they lacked the grace altogether, but that the present trials called for its full exercise; in suffering and delay, patience is indispensable. They mark the purpose: “that, after ye have done the will of God, ye might receive the promise” — endurance is the bridge between obedience and reward; having done God’s will, they must wait with patience to receive the promised inheritance. They observe Clarke’s balance: God furnishes the grace of patience, but the believer must exercise it; here they must be “workers together with God.” They note Calvin’s point that patience is needed not only because we must endure to the end, but because Satan harasses us with countless arts, so that without extraordinary patience we should be broken long before the goal. They observe that the promise is sure, but it is received not at once but after patient continuance in well-doing. The verse thus states the practical lesson of the encouragement: the readers need patience to persevere through trial, so that, having done God’s will, they may inherit the promise — for, as the next verse assures, the One who is to come will not tarry long.

So the witnesses see the writer name the grace the readers most need: patient endurance. The necessity: “ye have need of patience” — not that they lacked it altogether, but the present trials called for its full exercise; in suffering and delay, patience is indispensable. The purpose: “that, after ye have done the will of God, ye might receive the promise” — endurance is the bridge between obedience and reward. Clarke’s balance: God furnishes the grace, but the believer must exercise it. Calvin: patience is needed not only because we must endure to the end, but because Satan harasses us with countless arts. The promise is sure, but received after patient continuance in well-doing.
37:
On Hebrews 10:37 — “For yet a little while, and he that shall come will come, and will not tarry” — the witnesses mark the nearness of the Lord’s coming.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the citation. Robertson: “a very little while” (mikron hoson hoson, from Isaiah 26:20) introducing Habakkuk 2:3; “he that cometh” given a Messianic application. Adam Clarke applies it to the Messiah’s coming in judgment on the rebellious nation. The Reformed Calvin: nothing sustains us more than the hope of a near termination.

The verse encourages by the Lord’s nearness. In a very little while, the coming One will come and not delay. The shortness of the wait sustains endurance.

So the witnesses see the writer support the call to patience with the promise of the Lord’s speedy coming. The witnesses note the emphasis on shortness: “yet a little while” — literally “a very little while,” the doubled phrase stressing how brief the waiting is; the trial will not last long. They mark the assurance, drawn from Habakkuk: “he that shall come will come, and will not tarry” — the coming One (given a Messianic application) will surely come and will not delay beyond the appointed time. They observe the expositors’ range of reference: the coming of Christ in judgment on the unbelieving Jewish nation, soon to fall; and beyond that, his final advent to reward his people. They note Calvin’s point that nothing so sustains the fainting mind as the hope of a near end to the conflict, as a general cheers his soldiers with the prospect of soon-coming victory. They observe that the certainty and nearness of the Lord’s coming make patient endurance reasonable: the reward is not far off. The verse thus encourages perseverance by the promise of the speedy coming of the Lord, who will not tarry — setting up the great principle of the next verse, that the just shall live by faith and must not draw back.

So the witnesses see the writer support the call to patience with the promise of the Lord’s speedy coming. The emphasis on shortness: “yet a little while” — literally “a very little while,” the doubled phrase stressing how brief the waiting; the trial will not last long. The assurance from Habakkuk: “he that shall come will come, and will not tarry” — the coming One will surely come and not delay beyond the appointed time. The expositors’ range: Christ’s coming in judgment on the unbelieving Jewish nation, and beyond that his final advent. Calvin: nothing so sustains the fainting mind as the hope of a near end, as a general cheers his soldiers with soon-coming victory. The reward is not far off.
38:
On Hebrews 10:38 — “Now the just shall live by faith: but if any man draw back, my soul shall have no pleasure in him” — the witnesses mark the great principle of faith and the warning against drawing back.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the citation. Robertson: “if he shrink back” (hypisteiletai, to withdraw); the quotation of Habakkuk 2:4 as in Romans 1:17. Ellicott: probably “my righteous one shall live by faith.” Adam Clarke: the justified shall be preserved by faith. Wesley: he lives by faith “as long as he retains that gift… but if he draw back… I abhor him.”

The verse gives the rule of the righteous life and its opposite. The just lives by faith; the one who draws back God abhors. Faith sustains; apostasy forfeits God’s favor.

So the witnesses see the writer set the great principle of faith against the peril of drawing back. The witnesses note the famous declaration, quoted from Habakkuk and central to Paul as well: “the just shall live by faith” — the righteous, the justified, lives by faith; faith is the principle and sustenance of his whole spiritual life, by which he perseveres and is preserved. They mark the contrast: “but if any man draw back” — if he shrinks back, withdraws, apostatizes from the faith — “my soul shall have no pleasure in him” — God expresses his abhorrence; the one who turns away forfeits the divine favor. They observe Wesley’s sharp paraphrase: he lives by faith as long as he retains that gift, but if he draws back, God casts him off. They note that the two halves set the alternatives plainly before the wavering: to live by faith and endure, or to draw back and be rejected. They observe that this gathers up the whole exhortation: the call to patience and confidence rests on the rule that the righteous live by faith, while the drawer-back is abhorred. They note that the verse leads directly to the great chapter on faith that follows. The verse thus states the decisive principle and warning: the just live by faith, but the apostate God abhors — setting the readers, as the next verse declares, on the side of faith, not perdition.
39:
On Hebrews 10:39 — “But we are not of them who draw back unto perdition; but of them that believe to the saving of the soul” — the witnesses mark the confident conclusion.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the contrast. Robertson: “we” in contrast to renegades; “them that shrink back unto perdition” (hypostoles eis apoleian). Adam Clarke: “we are not the cowards, but the courageous.” Barnes: the apostle’s confidence that none of his readers would apostatize. Ellicott: “the gaining of the soul.”

The verse closes with confident hope. We are not of those who draw back to ruin, but of those who believe to save the soul. The writer places himself and his readers on the side of faith.

So the witnesses see the writer end the chapter with a confident, hopeful conclusion. The witnesses note the emphatic “we” — set in sharp contrast to the apostates who shrink back; the writer identifies himself with his readers on the better side. They mark the two destinies: “them who draw back unto perdition” — those who withdraw from the faith and so go to ruin and destruction; against “them that believe to the saving of the soul” — those who hold fast in faith and so attain the salvation of their souls. They observe Clarke’s rendering, “we are not the cowards, but the courageous” — the contrast is between the timid who retreat and the steadfast who persevere. They note Barnes’s point that the writer expresses confident hope that none of his readers would apostatize; the warning was real, but his expectation is good. They observe that this gracious conclusion both encourages the wavering — numbering them with the believing — and sets the final alternative: drawing back to perdition, or believing to the saving of the soul. They note that this leads straight into the catalogue of faith’s heroes in chapter 11, examples of the faith that saves. The verse thus closes the warning and exhortation on a note of hope: we belong not to the perishing apostates but to the persevering believers, whose faith saves the soul.

So the witnesses see the writer end the chapter with a confident, hopeful conclusion. The emphatic “we” — set in sharp contrast to the apostates who shrink back; the writer identifies himself with his readers on the better side. The two destinies: “them who draw back unto perdition” — those who withdraw from the faith and go to ruin; against “them that believe to the saving of the soul” — those who hold fast and attain salvation. Clarke’s rendering: “we are not the cowards, but the courageous.” Barnes: the writer expresses confident hope that none of his readers would apostatize. This gracious conclusion both encourages the wavering and leads into the catalogue of faith’s heroes in chapter 11.
